topic: Transformer architecture improvements in large language models 2020-2024
final_review:
The Transformer architecture has undergone significant improvements in large language models between 2020 and 2024, with a focus on enhancing their performance, efficiency, and applicability to various domains (Gao et al., 2023). One of the key areas of research has been the development of more efficient sequence modeling techniques. Another approach has been the use of retrieval-augmented generation, which has been surveyed by Gao et al. (2023) and shown to be effective in improving the performance of large language models.

In the medical domain, large language models have been applied to various tasks, including clinical text summarization (Van Veen et al., 2024), medical question answering (Singhal et al., 2025), and medical image segmentation (Wang et al., 2022). For example, Van Veen et al. (2024) demonstrated that adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization, while Singhal et al. (2025) showed that large language models can achieve expert-level performance in medical question answering.

The use of large language models in medical applications has also been surveyed, highlighting the potential benefits and challenges of using these models in healthcare. Furthermore, the role of AI in hospitals and clinics has been discussed, emphasizing the need for trustworthy and transparent AI systems in healthcare. In terms of explainability, there is a need for more effective and efficient methods.

There are also some contradictions and research gaps in the current literature. For example, while Gao et al. (2023) highlighted the benefits of retrieval-augmented generation, there is a need for further research on alternative approaches. Additionally, while Van Veen et al. (2024) demonstrated the effectiveness of large language models in clinical text summarization, Singhal et al. (2025) highlighted the need for more robust evaluation metrics for medical question answering.

In conclusion, the Transformer architecture has undergone significant improvements in large language models between 2020 and 2024, with a focus on enhancing their performance, efficiency, and applicability to various domains. However, there are still some contradictions and research gaps in the current literature, highlighting the need for further research and development in this area.

References:
Gao, Y., Xiong, Y., & Gao, X. (2023). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Large Language Models: A Survey. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2312.10997
Singhal, K. K., T., & Gottweis, J. (2025). Toward expert-level medical question answering with large language models. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-03423-7
Van Veen, D., V. U., C., & Blankemeier, L. (2024). Adapted large language models can outperform medical experts in clinical text summarization. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-024-02855-5
Wang, R., L., T., & Cui, R. (2022). Medical image segmentation using deep learning: A survey. https://doi.org/10.1049/ipr2.12419
